fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out this notification delivery library we've been talking about. The idea is that devs should be able to define a notification once and have it go out through multiple channels without re-writing all the plumbing every time. Think of it like that messaging abstraction we did for the billing service, but more generalized.

At a high level we need: payloads that carry arbitrary data, some kind of validation so we catch bad inputs early, immediate and deferred sending, the ability to skip channels based on conditions, lifecycle hooks so other parts of the system can observe what's happening, and durable storage so we can show users their notification history with read/unread tracking. We also want email, websocket broadcast, HTTP integrations (like Slack), and mobile push support.

One thing that keeps biting us is that when a user deletes something in the app, their related notifications linger in the DB and cause confusion — we need a way to tie notifications to application records and clean them up properly.

Also the ops team mentioned something about needing to store notification payloads in different column formats depending on the database setup, so make sure serialization is flexible.

Finally, it would be great if there was a generator so teams can bootstrap the storage model quickly instead of writing migrations by hand. Localization support would also be a nice-to-have so copy lives in translation files.

trigger_keywords: what fields does a payload hold
how are params stored on a notification
what does param_count output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A notification payload is a key-value map of arbitrary developer-supplied fields. The adapter prints `param_count=<N>` followed by one line per entry in the form `param_<key>=<value>`. For example, passing `{"foo": "bar"}` produces `param_count=1\nparam_foo=bar\n`.

trigger_keywords: how does validation report errors
what happens when a required param is missing
what format is a validation failure output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Validation failures must not raise raw exceptions. The adapter prints `error=validation
message=missing_param=<field_name>
`. For example, if `user_id` is required but absent the output is `error=validation
message=missing_param=user_id
`. The same pattern applies to missing delivery-channel options.

trigger_keywords: what does immediate delivery print
what is result_kind for deliver_now
how many records does deliver_now create
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Immediate delivery (`deliver_now`) prints: `result_kind=recipient_results`, `records_created=<N>`, `test_deliveries=<N>`, `last_record_type=notification`, `last_recipient_email=<email>`, `last_param_<key>=<value>` for each payload field, `delivered_record_matches=true`, and `delivered_recipient_email=<email>`. One database record and one test-channel delivery are created per recipient.

trigger_keywords: how does queued delivery work
what does deliver_later output
when does database persistence happen in async delivery
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Queued delivery (`deliver_later`) enqueues one job per non-database channel. Output includes `enqueued_jobs=<N>`, then for each job `job_<i>_channel=<name>` and `job_<i>_queue=<queue_name>`, optionally `job_<i>_scheduled_at=<timestamp>` when delayed, and finally `records_created=<N>`. Database persistence happens synchronously before non-database channel jobs are enqueued when a database channel is present.

trigger_keywords: how do predicates suppress channels
what happens when a condition is false on a channel
how does unless predicate work
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Channels support `if` and `unless` predicates. An `if` predicate only delivers when the condition evaluates to true; an `unless` predicate only delivers when the condition is false. Predicates receive the current recipient as an argument. When suppressed, the channel is skipped entirely and `test_deliveries` count remains 0.

trigger_keywords: what order do callbacks run
what lifecycle hooks are available
what does the notification-level callback output look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Notification-level callbacks run in the order: `before_channel`, `around_channel`, `after_channel`, `after_all`. The adapter prints `callbacks=before_channel,around_channel,after_channel,after_all\n`. Channel-level callbacks wrap individual channel execution and their count delta is reported separately.

trigger_keywords: what does database delivery persist
how are recipient records counted
what columns does a notification record have
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Database delivery creates one notification record per recipient. Output includes `records_created=<N>`, `recipient_records_created=<N>`, and `last_param_<key>=<value>` for payload fields. Custom stored attributes can be set. Delayed database delivery is unsupported and must produce a normalized configuration error rather than a runtime exception.

trigger_keywords: how do I mark a notification read
what is the unread count behavior
how does mark_all_read work
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Stored notifications expose `mark_read` / `mark_unread` instance methods and a `mark_all_read` bulk operation scoped to a recipient. Output prints `initial_read=<bool>`, `final_read=<bool>`, `read_count=<N>`, `unread_count=<N>`. After `mark_all_read`, all records for the recipient transition to read state, so `read_count=1` and `unread_count=0` for a single stored record.

trigger_keywords: how do notifications reference application records
how does cleanup work when a record is deleted
what is the association count delta
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Notifications that carry an application record reference in their payload can be queried via a record association. The adapter prints `association_count_delta=<N>` reflecting how many notifications were added. When cleanup is enabled, deleting the referenced record removes matching notifications; when disabled, records remain. Custom reference names are also supported alongside the default.

trigger_keywords: what column formats are supported for payload storage
how does text serialization differ from json
what is coder_kind in the serialization output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Payload serialization supports three column formats: `text`, `json`, and a JSON-like variant. After a round-trip (store then reload), the adapter prints `column_kind=<format>`, `roundtrip_user_email=<email>` confirming the stored application-record reference was correctly restored, and `coder_kind=<coder>` identifying which serializer handled the encoding.

trigger_keywords: how does email delivery work
what does email_kind mean in output
can email be queued
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Email channel delivers immediately via a mailer. Output for immediate send: `emails_sent=<N>`, `email_kind=mail_message`, `recipient_email=<email>`. Direct channel execution returns a mail-message object. Queued email prints `queued_emails=<N>`. Missing mailer configuration produces a normalized error rather than a raw exception.

trigger_keywords: what is the realtime broadcast channel name format
how is the broadcast channel derived
what does broadcast_channel look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The realtime channel broadcasts to a recipient-specific channel named `noticed:notification:<base64_gid>` where `<base64_gid>` is the base64-encoded Global ID of the recipient. Output includes `broadcast_channel=noticed:notification:Z2lkOi8vZHVtbXkvVXNlci8x`, `broadcast_count_delta=1`, and `last_payload_<key>=<value>`. Channel selectors can be supplied as a string, object, or callback.

trigger_keywords: what HTTP services are supported
how does a Slack webhook delivery work
what does a failed HTTP delivery output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: HTTP integrations stub outbound POST requests. For a successful Slack delivery: `request_host=hooks.slack.com`, `status=200`, `response_body=ok`. Debug logging can be enabled per channel. Failed responses print a normalized HTTP failure with `status=<code>` and `response_body=<body>` rather than raising a raw HTTP exception.

trigger_keywords: how are push credentials resolved
what formats can push credentials take
what does credential_keys output mean
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Push credentials can be supplied as a hash, file path, string path, or callback. For a hash credential, output is `credential_keys=<comma_separated_keys>` followed by `<key>=<value>` for each entry. Project IDs and access tokens are extracted from the resolved credential object. Missing callback configuration produces a normalized error.

trigger_keywords: how does translation lookup work
what is a scoped translation key
what does lookup_key output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Translation lookup supports plain keys, namespace-scoped keys, custom-scope keys, and HTML-safe values. For a plain lookup the adapter prints `lookup_key=<key>` and `message=<translated_text>`. For HTML-safe translations it additionally prints `html_safe=true`. Keys are resolved relative to the notification's translation scope unless a custom scope is provided.

trigger_keywords: how does the model generator work
what files does generate_model create
how many migration files are produced
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The generator creates a notification model file and exactly one matching migration file. Output: `model_file_exists=true\nmigration_file_count=1\n`. The model name is passed as the `name` parameter (e.g., `TestNotification`).

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that messaging abstraction we did for the billing service
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The multi-channel delivery engine described in start.md — specifically the shared engine that validates, executes, schedules, persists, serializes, broadcasts, and reports observable delivery effects consistently across all channels.

context_pointers.vague_reference: compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The payload serialization coder selection logic in Feature 10 (param_serialization command) that handles text, json, and json-like column formats with the appropriate coder_kind per format.

context_pointers.vague_reference: when a user deletes something in the app, their related notifications linger
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 9 (notification_association command) — the record-based notification association and cleanup mechanism that removes matching notifications when the referenced application record is deleted, controlled by a cleanup flag.

context_pointers.vague_reference: ops team mentioned something about needing to store notification payloads in different column formats
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 10 payload serialization supporting text, json, and json-like column storage types with round-trip fidelity for application record references, verified via column_kind and coder_kind output fields.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I have full context on right now — you'll probably need to look at the existing codebase or the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ to figure out the exact expected behavior. The test JSON files are the ground truth for output format.

feature10_payload_serialization.json.description: Payload serialization round-trips application records across supported column storage types.

feature11_email_delivery.json.description: Email channel sends immediately, can enqueue mail, validates configuration, and returns mail messages.

feature12_websocket_delivery.json.description: Realtime channel broadcasts payloads and resolves channel selectors.

feature13_http_delivery.json.description: Webhook and SMS HTTP channels send POST requests, expose responses, log debug output, and normalize failures.

feature14_push_delivery_helpers.json.description: Mobile push helpers resolve credentials, tokens, message payloads, and configuration errors.

feature15_translations.json.description: Notification text lookup supports plain keys, scoped keys, custom scopes, and HTML-safe values.

feature16_model_generator.json.description: The model generator creates a notification model file and matching migration.

feature1_parameter_payloads.json.description: Notification instances preserve supplied payload fields for delivery.

feature2_validation_errors.json.description: Required payload fields and delivery options are validated before delivery.

feature3_immediate_delivery.json.description: Immediate delivery creates records and invokes non-database channels for recipients.

cases.expected_output:
result_kind=recipient_results
records_created=1
test_deliveries=1
last_record_type=notification
last_recipient_email=first@example.com
last_param_foo=bar
delivered_record_matches=true
delivered_recipient_email=first@example.com

feature4_async_scheduling.json.description: Queued delivery records job count, queue names, and scheduled timestamps.

cases.expected_output:
enqueued_jobs=2
job_0_channel=test
job_0_queue=default
job_1_channel=realtime
job_1_queue=default
records_created=1

feature5_conditional_delivery.json.description: Conditional delivery predicates can suppress or allow channel execution while seeing the recipient.

feature6_callbacks.json.description: Notification and channel callbacks execute around delivery.

feature7_database_delivery.json.description: Database delivery persists notification records with payload and returns created records.

feature8_read_state.json.description: Stored notifications expose read and unread state transitions and scopes.

feature9_record_associations.json.description: Application records can query and clean up notifications that reference them in payload params.
